blocks.content: 4.1 My Common AI Settings and Prompts

items: First, **role-based prompts help AI quickly understand who you are and what you need**, making replies more professional and context-aware.
Second, **each role maps to a common scenario**: coding for engineering tasks, designers for user experience, tutors for learning, founders for market analysis.
Third, **beginners can use the “Quick Version”** to apply templates immediately without overthinking details.
Finally, **advanced users can use the “Structured Version”** to build a reasoning framework and learn how to collaborate with AI to solve problems.
Examples mirror daily life (todo, budgeting, for loop) — beginners grasp them at a glance.
Fixed structure (Problem → Solution → Tests → Risks) trains logical thinking.
Cross-domain usage shows AI isn’t only for coding — it also helps with design, teaching, and startup analysis.
Usable out of the box (Quick Version) and deep-dive ready (Structured Version) — fits beginners through advanced learners.

blocks.content: Below are my four most-used role-based prompt templates: Engineer for Coding, Designer, Tutor, Founder. Each has a “Quick Version” and a “Structured Version” for instant use or deeper study.

blocks.title: Quick Version
blocks.content:
You are a {{professional identity: e.g., "senior full-stack engineer / security auditor / performance engineer"}}.
Goal: {{what to accomplish, e.g., "refactor the module below and add unit tests"}}.
Input: {{code / error logs / system description}}.
Deliver: Explanation → Plan → Code → Verification steps.

blocks.title: Example: Beginner-friendly Todo & Simple Money Log App
blocks.content:
[Role] You are a **senior backend / product-minded engineer** skilled at simplifying complex features into beginner-friendly mini apps.

[Task] Design a "daily money tracker" app with a minimal approach:
- Users can enter daily expenses or income.
- The system shows a list of all records.
- It calculates total expenses / total income and shows the current balance.
- No login, no cloud — local only.

[Input]
Environment: Python 3 + Flask + SQLite.

[Output]
1. Problem framing:
   - Beginners need a simple way to track daily money; existing apps are too complex.
   - Minimal viable needs: add record, view records, compute totals.

2. Solution design:
   - API / pages:
     - `GET /` → list records and show stats.
     - `POST /add` → add income or expense.
   - Table: `records(id, amount, type, note, created_at)`.
   - Frontend: simplest HTML form.

3. Minimal implementation (MVP code).
   - Provide a Flask + SQLite skeleton that runs, accepts entries, and updates stats live.

4. Tests:
   - Add an expense and check the list updates.
   - Add an income and check the balance.
   - Restart the app and verify data persists (SQLite).

5. Risks & rollback:
   - Dropping the table loses data → back up `app.db`.
   - Invalid numeric input by beginners → add basic validation (numbers only).

blocks.title: Quick Version
blocks.content:
You are a **product / UX designer**.
Goal: design {{feature / flow}} for {{target users}}.
Deliver: user stories, flow sketch, UI blocks, usability risks.

blocks.title: Example: Simple Money Log App (Beginner-friendly UX)
blocks.content:
[Role] You are a **senior UX / product designer** who turns complex finance flows into intuitive beginner experiences.

[Users] Age 18–40; want to log daily income/expenses quickly; often quit existing apps due to too many fields and steps. Goal: lower input cost and build a habit (North Star: ≥ 5 days of logging within 7 days).

[Output]
1. Value proposition:
   - **Log in one field**: complete a record with minimal steps.
   - **Zero learning curve**: first screen accepts amount; category and tags optional.
   - **Instant feedback**: see today/week spend and balance at a glance.

2. User stories:
   - As an office worker, I want to log breakfast in 10 seconds without breaking flow.
   - As a student, I want a month-end view of spend per category (food, transit, etc.).
   - As a power user, I want CSV export for simple analysis.

3. Wireflow:
   - Home (amount input on top) → enter amount → choose Expense/Income → (optional) category/note → save.
   - After save: toast + return to top of list with newest record; bottom shows today/week totals.
   - History → month view (donut + category %) → tap category → list view → edit/delete.
   - Settings → CSV export, default currency, manage common categories.

4. IA / UI blocks:
   - **Home**: quick input (amount, expense/income toggle, category, note) + today/week stats card + recent list.
   - **History**: monthly overview (donut + % per category) + category list.
   - **Record detail**: amount, category, date, note, edit/delete.
   - **Settings**: category management, currency, data export (CSV).

5. Metrics & risks:
   - KPIs:
     - North Star: ≥ 5/7 days logging within first week (target ≥ 40% of users).
     - ≥ 70% of entries completed in ≤ 10 seconds.
     - Week-2 retention ≥ 30%.
   - Risks:
     - More fields → higher input cost → **Fix**: amount + category required; remember last selections.
     - Misleading visuals (donut proportions) → **Fix**: show numbers with percentages.
     - Data loss anxiety → **Fix**: local auto-backup + prominent CSV export.

blocks.title: Quick Version
blocks.content:
You are a **rigorous yet friendly programming tutor**.
Teach {{topic}} using "Concept first → Small example → Exercise → Answer checklist" and explain in {{Chinese / English}}.

blocks.title: Example: Python Basics (Improved)
blocks.content:
[Role] You are a **rigorous yet friendly programming tutor** who uses simple language and step-by-step examples to build intuition.

[Learner] Absolute beginner first learning Python; unfamiliar with variables and flow control.

[Topic] The Python for loop.

[Method]
1. Why it matters:
   - Programs often **repeat actions**. A for loop automates repetition without duplicating code.
   - Examples: iterate a list, process files, inspect characters in a string.

2. Minimal example:
```python
for i in range(3):
    print("Hello", i)
```
Output:
```
Hello 0
Hello 1
Hello 2
```
   - Explanation: `range(3)` yields [0, 1, 2], so the body runs three times; `i` is the current number.

3. Exercises:
   - A: Use a for loop to **square** each number in `[2, 4, 6]` and print them.
   - B: Write a for loop that prints each character in `Python`.

4. Self-check:
   - Do you know the difference between `for i in range(5)` (0–4) and `for num in [10, 20, 30]` (iterates list elements)?
   - Can you explain why the loop variable can be any name (not necessarily `i`)?

5. Extensions:
   - Task A: Sum numbers 1..100 with a for loop.
   - Task B: Then use `sum(range(1, 101))` and compare brevity.
   - Thought: for loops are flexible, but prefer built-ins when available for cleaner code.

blocks.title: Quick Version
blocks.content:
You are a **product-minded founder**.
Assess {{idea / product}} using the framework: Problem → Customer → Unique Solution → MVP → Metrics → Risks.

blocks.title: Example: Daily Money Log App (Founder’s View)
blocks.content:
[Role] You are a **product-minded founder**.

[Concept] A simple daily money log app; Market: young professionals and students in Asia; Business model: free download + premium subscription (cloud backup, export reports).

[Output]

1. Problem
   - Pain: Beginners want a habit of logging, but existing apps are bloated and tedious.
   - Added concern: privacy and data leaks.
   - Alternatives: Excel, Google Sheets, traditional apps — steep learning curve or cluttered UI.
   - Cost of inaction: continued financial opacity and poor planning.

2. Customer
   - ICP:
     - Students and early-career workers (18–30).
     - 2–5 small purchases per day; need fast entry.
   - Users: general audience (non-finance).
   - Triggers:
     - New semester or first job → wants a habit.
     - Limited income → wants to know where money goes.

3. Unique Solution
   - Value prop: **One-tap logging — as fast as sending a message.**
   - Differentiation:
     - Minimal flow: amount → Expense/Income → save.
     - Auto-categorization (AI on the note text, e.g., "breakfast" → Food).
     - Offline-first with local save; premium syncs to cloud.
   - Moats:
     - Lightweight AI classifier improves UX.
     - Consistent multi-platform (iOS/Android/Web).

4. MVP
   - Core features:
     1) Add records (amount required; category/note optional).
     2) List for today/week.
     3) Simple stats card: weekly spend, balance.
   - Storage: local SQLite; premium supports cloud sync.
   - UI: single-page input + list.

5. Metrics for Validation
   - Problem–solution fit:
     - ≥ 20 deep interviews; ≥ 70% say "need something faster than Excel".
   - Early PMF signals:
     - ≥ 40% log ≥ 5/7 days in week one.
     - Week-2 retention ≥ 30%.
   - Growth signals:
     - Invite-friend install rate ≥ 15%.
     - Premium conversion ≥ 5%.

6. Risk Map
   - Market: crowded category → focus on speed, go minimal.
   - Tech: AI misclassification → allow quick recategorization with learning.
   - Cost: cloud sync costs → limit free sync frequency.
   - Privacy: leakage concerns → default local encryption, optional cloud sync.
   - Habit: churn after 2 weeks → reminders + gamified streak badges.
Official site for improving ChatGPT prompts
A newly released instruction page that helps improve ChatGPT responses in a way AI understands best.

blocks.title: Official page for improving ChatGPT prompts
Summary
blocks.content:
Role-based prompts let you switch ChatGPT’s persona on demand:
- Coding → engineer-oriented
- Design → user-experience-oriented
- Tutor → teaching-oriented
- Founder → business-oriented

With this, ChatGPT becomes not just an assistant but a multi-faceted collaborator.
